identity.title: Frozen Fruit, Juice, and Vegetable Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market processors that manufacture frozen fruits, frozen vegetables, and frozen fruit juices, drinks, cocktail mixes, or concentrates for repeat retail, foodservice, distributor, industrial-ingredient, and private-label customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Frozen prepared meals and specialty foods, seafood, frozen bakery or dairy products, fresh-only produce handlers, captive internal plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat physical-product sales by pound, case, shipment, contract pack, or seasonal program, commonly through private-label, branded, foodservice, industrial ingredient, and distributor channels.

a.bridge: BLS reports broader food-manufacturing occupations and Census reports an upper-bound exposure of manufacturing workers to all advanced automating technologies, not the remaining AI-exposed tasks in frozen produce. I allocate exposure to grading and vision-inspection triage, crop and demand forecasting, line scheduling, cold-chain monitoring, traceability documentation, maintenance prioritization, and administration, while discounting physical receiving, washing, cutting, blanching, freezing, sanitation, packaging intervention, and warehouse movement. The all-technology proxy likely overstates AI-only exposure.
a.rationale: Core operations are capital- and equipment-intensive, seasonal, cold-chain dependent, and physically regulated. AI can reduce cognitive and inspection labor around the process but cannot replace most line, sanitation, refrigeration, maintenance, and material-handling tasks over five years.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted staffing matrix plus task-level time studies at independent frozen fruit, juice, and vegetable plants, including current machine-vision and planning automation, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for all food manufacturing, not 311411.
Advanced-technology exposure includes robots, dedicated equipment, cloud systems, and non-AI software.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI use across nonfarm businesses and ABS measures workforce effects of several technologies across industries rather than implementation of exposed tasks in frozen produce plants. I translate size-skewed business adoption into five-year implementation and reduce it for seasonal crop variability, sparse labeled quality data, food-safety validation, legacy controls, refrigeration uptime, and LMM integration capacity. Any-function AI use likely overstates production-grade implementation.
rho.rationale: Office, forecasting, scheduling, and documentation uses are deployable, but plant-floor quality and process decisions must operate reliably under biological input variation and regulatory controls. Implementation should capture only a minority of the exposed opportunity in the base case.
rho.change_evidence: Verified labor-hour and error-rate outcomes from AI vision, yield optimization, predictive maintenance, warehouse scheduling, and traceability deployments at smaller frozen-produce processors would move the range.
caveats: Census AI definitions and reference periods changed.
Adoption rates do not measure captured labor opportunity and may be dominated by low-risk office uses.

e.rationale: The official code is centered on operating manufacturers selling repeat physical output to external customers, so most true LMM firms should fit. Deductions cover captive facilities, cooperatives or ownership structures not open to control transfer, businesses outside the normalized EBITDA band after seasonal adjustment, extreme customer concentration, and plants lacking transferable supply or food-safety systems.
e.change_evidence: A candidate-level review of the 46 frozen firms using ownership, audited normalized EBITDA, customer and grower concentration, captive status, certifications, facility condition, and revenue-channel data would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source directly measures eligibility among the frozen 46-firm estimate.
The common food-processing margin bridge may misclassify capital-intensive or volatile seasonal processors.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveys five-year transfer intent across US business owners and finds 17% among owners age 55 or older versus 10% among younger owners; Project Equity reports 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age. I blend those cross-industry signals and discount gifts, internal reorganizations, closures, withdrawn listings, and failed financing. Older manufacturing ownership may overstate completed qualifying transfers, while observed sector divestitures show assets and co-manufacturing arrangements can transfer.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership supports a recurring pipeline, but frozen-produce plants require heavy seasonal working capital, specialized refrigeration assets, grower relationships, and environmental and food-safety diligence. Those constraints prevent many intended exits from becoming qualifying control deals.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, IBBA, and food-sector advisers filtered to frozen produce and the target EBITDA band, divided by verified eligible firms, would materially revise s5.
caveats: Owner-intention and age evidence does not isolate NAICS 311411.
Public-company product-line divestitures are not representative of LMM whole-company transfers.

q.bridge: Seneca Foods sells packaged fruit and vegetables across private-label, foodservice, branded co-pack, and industrial channels and states that competition is substantial and excess capacity depresses selling prices. It is a larger, canned-heavy public company, not an LMM pure frozen-produce processor or a measurement of retained AI benefits. I infer below-majority retention for commodity and private-label work, with the proxy potentially understating differentiated frozen-fruit or specialty-ingredient retention and overstating scale-based bargaining power.
q.rationale: Per-pound and per-case pricing permits initial retention, but retailer and foodservice bargaining, annual bids, transparent commodity inputs, co-pack negotiations, and excess capacity should pass through much of a generalized cost advantage over five years. Proprietary sourcing, quality consistency, capacity scarcity, and specialized formats support the upper range.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, annual bid histories, price-cost waterfalls, capacity-utilization records, customer concentration, and gross margins by private-label, branded, foodservice, and ingredient channel at independent processors would materially move q.
caveats: Seneca's frozen vegetables were only 8% of its fiscal 2025 food-packaging sales.
Competition disclosures do not directly quantify pass-through of AI savings.

d5.bridge: USDA ERS measures aggregate vegetable availability and frozen-vegetable trade, not constant-price demand for all output of NAICS 311411. Per-capita vegetable availability reached a 35-year low in 2024 and processing vegetables declined substantially since 1996, while frozen-vegetable import volume and export volume rose in 2024. I combine the structural per-capita decline with population and mixed trade demand to anchor a modest five-year quantity decline. Vegetable-heavy evidence may understate frozen-fruit or juice trends, and import growth does not directly equal domestic-manufacturer demand.
d5.rationale: Frozen produce benefits from convenience, year-round availability, foodservice, private label, and reduced spoilage, but long-run processing-vegetable availability is declining and imports compete with domestic output. Population and export channels limit downside, while category and crop mix create a wide range.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NIQ unit sales, USDA cold-storage disappearance, processor shipment pounds, and constant-price forecasts split into frozen fruit, non-potato vegetables, potatoes, juices, and foodservice versus retail would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Availability is a supply-disappearance proxy, not consumption or manufacturer demand.
The NAICS includes fruit, juice, and vegetables, while the strongest evidence is vegetable-focused.

o.rationale: Freezing, grading, food-safety control, traceability, cold storage, packaging, and delivery are physical regulated functions that cannot be supplied entirely by software. Customers may vertically integrate, switch to fresh, canned, or imported finished product, or source directly from larger processors, but these paths change the supplier rather than eliminate accountable processing for most retained frozen quantity.
o.change_evidence: Retailer and foodservice make-versus-buy surveys, processor-customer churn records, vertical-integration announcements, import penetration by finished form, and co-manufacturing contract data would test bypass risk.
caveats: Industry consolidation can remove demand from LMM operators without eliminating the processing function.
Imports may shift the accountable operator outside the US and are only indirectly reflected in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Frozen fruit, juice, and vegetable manufacturing offers repeat, operator-required demand and a meaningful estimated LMM population, but the labor base is largely physical and commercial retention is constrained by commodity inputs, private-label bidding, and excess capacity. Structural demand evidence is softer than convenience-oriented category narratives suggest.
narrative.ai_impact: AI is most relevant to crop and demand forecasting, production scheduling, vision-inspection triage, yield analysis, maintenance prioritization, cold-chain alerts, traceability documents, and administration. Receiving, washing, cutting, blanching, freezing, sanitation, packaging interventions, refrigeration maintenance, and warehouse movement remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Per-case and per-pound pricing lets a processor retain benefits initially, but annual customer bids, retailer and foodservice leverage, transparent crop and freight costs, and available capacity share savings. Specialized formats, dependable seasonal capacity, sourcing relationships, and quality performance support better retention.
narrative.firm_availability: Most firms in the code are operating manufacturers and should fit the recurring-customer lens, subject to captive status, ownership structure, concentration, certification, and normalized seasonal economics. Aging manufacturing ownership creates potential transfer supply, but asset intensity and working-capital needs reduce completion.
narrative.demand_durability: Freezing remains necessary for year-round availability and low-spoilage distribution, yet USDA data show a long decline in processing-vegetable availability. Recent trade volumes are mixed-to-positive, so a modest real quantity decline is more defensible than either collapse or strong growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are crop and weather volatility, power and refrigeration costs, food-safety incidents, environmental compliance, import competition, labor seasonality, customer and grower concentration, and low capacity utilization. Evidence is weak on six-digit tasks, private contracts, deal completion, and separate fruit, juice, and vegetable demand.
narrative.principal_driver: Physical freezing and cold-chain services remain necessary for recurring retail, foodservice, private-label, and ingredient demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity-like bidding and excess capacity limit retained value while long-run processing-vegetable availability is declining.

sources.title: 311411: Frozen Fruit, Juice, and Vegetable Manufacturing - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Official industry definition and 218 employer establishments in 2023.

sources.title: Food Manufacturing: NAICS 311
sources.supports: Broader food-manufacturing employment and occupation mix, including production, batchmaking, packaging, and supervisory roles.

sources.title: Food Processing Equipment Workers
sources.supports: Physical production duties, machine tending, cleaning and lifting requirements, and use of automatic weighing and mixing equipment.

sources.title: Three Results From Recent Research on Advanced Technology Use and Automation
sources.supports: Manufacturing workers' 52% upper-bound exposure to advanced automating technologies versus 28% outside manufacturing.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS AI use of 17-20% overall in late 2025 through May 2026, expected use of 20-23%, and higher adoption at larger firms.

sources.title: Census Bureau's 2023 Annual Business Survey Provides Insight into Technology Adoption by Businesses
sources.supports: Most adopters reported no overall workforce-number change and frequently experienced workflow or skill effects.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Five-year sale or transfer plans reported by 17% of owners age 55 or older and 10% of younger owners in fall 2024.

sources.title: Employee Ownership for Manufacturers
sources.supports: Reports 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age.

sources.title: B&G Foods Business Update
sources.supports: 2026 Green Giant US frozen business divestiture and continuing co-manufacturing arrangement; identifies frozen operations as capital-intensive, seasonal, and working-capital heavy.

sources.title: Seneca Foods Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Frozen-vegetable share, private-label and foodservice channels, substantial competition, and downward selling-price pressure from excess industry capacity.

sources.title: Vegetable Availability Declined in 2024
sources.supports: US vegetable availability fell to 376 pounds per person in 2024, a 35-year low; processing vegetables declined 32 pounds from 1996 to 2024.

sources.title: Vegetables and Pulses Outlook: April 2025
sources.supports: Frozen-vegetable import value and volume and processed-vegetable export value and volume in 2024, including product-mix detail.
